title: Fitting a mortise deadlock to an exterior door
subtitle: Cut a mortise in a timber door stile and install a 5-lever deadlock with matching striking plate for a secure front door
excerpt: How to fit a 5-lever mortise deadlock to an exterior timber door. Covers marking the mortise position, chiselling the pocket, drilling the keyhole, and fitting the striking plate to the door frame.
sourceNotes: UK building trade and locksmith practice. British Standard BS 3621 covers 5-lever deadlocks for insurance-approved security. Newey and Drage, Practical Carpentry and Joinery (1905, public domain).

glossaryTerms.definition: A deadlock in which the bolt is released only by a key that lifts all five levers to the correct height simultaneously before the bolt can slide. The 5-lever standard is the minimum required by most UK household insurers on exterior doors. Deadlocks have a bolt that shoots fully into the striking plate and cannot be pushed back from the outside without a key (unlike a latch, which can be slipped with a credit card. The BS 3621 standard specifies the pick and key-duplication resistance required for insurance approval.

content.text: is the lock most home insurers require on a front door. Chisel a slot into the door edge, drill the keyhole, fit the lock body flush, and align the striking plate on the frame.

Position the lock body on the door edge at the mid-point of the door height) typically 1000 mm from the floor. Trace around the forend (the flat face plate of the lock body) on the door edge with a sharp knife. Mark the mortise depth from the edge using the lock body width as a gauge. The mortise must be centred on the door stile thickness, not too close to either face.

Drill a series of overlapping holes the full depth of the mortise using a bit matched to the lock body width. Chop out the remaining timber between the holes with a sharp chisel, working with the grain. Test the lock body for fit (it should slide in with hand pressure and sit fully below the door edge. Pare the mortise clean and to depth until the forend sits flush with the door edge.

With the lock body in the mortise, mark the keyhole positions on both faces of the door from the lock body keyhole. Drill through on each mark using the bit size specified in the lock instructions. Use a keyhole saw or jigsaw to elongate the lower hole into the keyhole teardrop profile. Fit the keyhole escutcheons on both faces.

Close the door and operate the lock key to shoot the bolt; mark the bolt position on the door frame. Chisel a pocket for the striking plate box on the frame. Set the striking plate over the pocket and mark the recess for the forend plate. Chisel the recess so the plate sits flush with the frame. Fix with the screws provided. Test that the bolt shoots fully and the door cannot be forced open when locked.

attrs.symptom: The bolt does not reach the striking plate box fully) only half the bolt engages
attrs.cause: The striking plate box is not aligned with the bolt path, or the mortise is not deep enough and the lock body sits proud of centre.
attrs.fix: Mark the bolt position again carefully and enlarge the striking plate box in the direction the bolt is falling short. Check that the lock body forend is fully flush with the door edge before re-testing.
